topicArea: Names, Renaming & Who Holds the Pen

questions.passage:
The form allows a few weeks at most. Somewhere in the first days of a life, before the person has done anything at all, others select the word that will be shouted across playgrounds, printed on diplomas, and muttered by receptionists for the better part of a century. The first official act of every biography is performed by someone else, and the arrangement is so universal that its strangeness has to be recovered deliberately: we live for decades inside a decision we were not consulted on, and most of us never leave it, not because the exits are locked but because the word has grown into the face.

A name is routinely called a label, which understates the job. Labels describe contents; names summon persons. To be named is to be addressable, callable across a room, indictable in a court, inscribable on a list, and the conventions by which names are assigned carry a society's structure in miniature: patronymics announcing whose child, birth-order names announcing which child, saints' calendars announcing when, clan names announcing to whom you owe and who owes you. Before the bearer has a biography, the name has filed her, and the filing is legible to every stranger who hears it. The file can be misread, of course, and often is; but even the misreading is work the name performs, an introduction delivered before the person can speak.

The filing runs deeper than administrative convenience, because names that officially mean nothing still say a great deal. A name carries era, region, class, and ethnicity in its syllables, and listeners act on the information: studies circulating identical résumés under different names have repeatedly found that the name alone moves the callback rate. The name, one might say, arrives with luggage the bearer never packed, and she will carry it through every introduction, however thoroughly her conduct contradicts the freight.

Because names do so much, controlling them has always been worth someone's trouble. Institutions that wished to own people began by renaming them: enslavers assigned names, residential schools confiscated them, immigration clerks amputated the unpronounceable, and each act announced, more efficiently than any decree, who now held the pen. The traffic runs the other way as well. The convert, the artist, the woman shedding a marriage, the person remaking a life all reach for the same instrument, and self-renaming remains the most compact declaration of self-ownership a person can make, which is precisely why regimes of control have so often priced it out of reach or forbidden it outright: the biography's first official act, performed at last by its subject.

Between strangers and intimates, meanwhile, names run a quieter economy. The full name on the summons, the first name from the new colleague, the diminutive permitted to a few, the childhood nickname that only one voice still uses: each form marks a distance, and unauthorized shortening is trespass, however friendly its intent. Refusing someone's chosen form, insisting on the maiden name, the discarded name, the formal name after being offered the familiar one, is never a slip of memory only; it is a position in an argument about who the person is, conducted through vocatives, and both parties understand the terms perfectly, whatever politeness pretends.

The ideal of self-naming, though, meets a structural limit that its celebrants sometimes skip past: a name is for others to use. Its whole function is performed in other mouths, and a name no one will say has failed as a name, whatever it means to its bearer. Naming is therefore never purely self-expression; it is a standing negotiation between a bearer and a community of speakers, which is why states maintain registries and some countries approved-name lists, why courts referee disputed changes, and why the community's cooperation, freely given or legally compelled, remains the medium in which every name lives or dies.

The infant's form, then, was never the trivial paperwork it appeared to be. To name is to admit into address, and every subsequent contest over the word, the confiscations, the reclamations, the refusals at the office, the petitions at the courthouse, is a contest over the same two questions the form quietly settled the first time: who holds the pen, and how gracefully the community re-signs when the bearer, at last consulted, asks it to.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Names file their bearers and function only in others' mouths, so naming is an ongoing negotiation over who holds the pen, neither trivial label nor pure self-expression.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because résumé studies show names alone move callback rates, parents should deliberately choose names that conceal their children's origins from employers and admissions committees.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The history of institutional renaming proves that all naming is an act of ownership that self-renaming can never meaningfully reverse.

choices.label: D
choices.text: States should abolish approved-name lists and registries, since these bureaucratic instruments prevent names from performing their social functions.

explanations.A: Correct. The passage rejects the label view, names summon rather than describe, shows the name filing its bearer with luggage never packed, tracks renaming as the pen changing hands in both directions, and then imposes the structural limit: a name is for others to use, so naming is a standing negotiation between bearer and community. The conclusion gathers this into the two questions, who holds the pen and how gracefully the community re-signs.
explanations.B: This choice converts the third paragraph's evidence into parental advice the passage never gives. The résumé studies establish that names carry consequential freight; no strategy of concealment is proposed, and the passage's interest is in the naming relation, not in optimizing outcomes.
explanations.C: This choice flattens the fourth paragraph's two-way traffic into one direction. Institutional renaming announces who holds the pen, but self-renaming is credited as the most compact declaration of self-ownership a person can make, a meaningful reversal the choice denies.
explanations.D: This choice inverts the sixth paragraph's account of registries and lists. These appear as consequences of naming's negotiated, community-dependent character, evidence that names live in others' cooperation, not as obstacles the passage campaigns to remove.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fourth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: catalog the administrative errors and accidental truncations that immigration clerks introduced into the family names of arriving migrants.

choices.label: B
choices.text: argue that religious conversion is the only legitimate occasion for changing one's name.

choices.label: C
choices.text: show that control of names runs in both directions: imposed renaming announces ownership, self-renaming declares self-ownership.

choices.label: D
choices.text: explain why patronymics and clan names eventually gave way to modern registry systems.

explanations.A: This choice reduces one item in the paragraph's catalog to its whole point. The clerk's amputations join the enslavers' assignments and the schools' confiscations as instances of a single claim, renaming announces who holds the pen, and the paragraph's second half reverses the direction entirely.
explanations.B: This choice elevates one member of a list into a rule the paragraph never states. The convert appears alongside the artist, the divorcing woman, and the person remaking a life, all reaching for the same instrument, with no ranking of legitimacy among them.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph opens with the premise, because names do so much, controlling them has always been worth someone's trouble, then runs the traffic both ways: institutions that wished to own people began by renaming them, and self-renaming remains the most compact declaration of self-ownership, the biography's first official act performed at last by its subject.
explanations.D: This choice invents a historical transition the passage never narrates. Patronymics and clan names belong to the second paragraph's conventions, registries to the sixth paragraph's negotiation, and the fourth paragraph connects neither; its subject is power over names, not their administrative evolution.

questions.stem: The phrase arrives with luggage the bearer never packed, as used in the third paragraph, most nearly means that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: children inherit legal obligations from the relatives whose names they are given.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a name carries social information, class, era, ethnicity, that others act on regardless of the bearer's conduct.

choices.label: C
choices.text: immigrants historically lost material possessions along with the syllables that busy clerks removed from their family names.

choices.label: D
choices.text: names accumulate nicknames and diminutives that the bearer cannot control once they spread.

explanations.A: This choice literalizes the luggage into legal inheritance. The paragraph's freight is informational, the era, region, class, and ethnicity legible in syllables, and the acting parties are listeners like the résumé screeners, not creditors enforcing family obligations.
explanations.B: Correct. The paragraph states that names that officially mean nothing still say a great deal, carrying era, region, class, and ethnicity, and that listeners act on the information, the callback studies being the proof. The luggage is this unchosen social freight, carried through every introduction however thoroughly the bearer's conduct contradicts it.
explanations.C: This choice attaches the metaphor to the wrong paragraph and reads it materially besides. The clerks' amputations belong to the fourth paragraph's account of power; the luggage names what an intact name carries, not what a truncated one lost.
explanations.D: This choice mistakes the fifth paragraph's intimacy economy for the third's freight. Diminutives mark distance between speakers; the luggage is the demographic information packed into the name at birth, present before any nickname forms.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward institutions that renamed the people they controlled is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: neutral, presenting imposed renaming as one administrative convention among the many the second paragraph catalogs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: condemning, treating assigned and confiscated names as announcements of ownership more efficient than any decree.

choices.label: C
choices.text: understanding, since institutions processing many people required standardized names to function.

choices.label: D
choices.text: skeptical that the renamings had lasting effects, since bearers were free to resume their names later.

explanations.A: This choice files the fourth paragraph's examples under the second's neutral conventions, but the passage separates them deliberately: patronymics and calendars carry structure, while enslavers, residential schools, and confiscation belong to a paragraph about institutions that wished to own people, vocabulary that is anything but neutral.
explanations.B: Correct. The paragraph's terms carry the verdict: institutions that wished to own people began by renaming them, schools confiscated names, clerks amputated them, and each act announced, more efficiently than any decree, who now held the pen. Ownership, confiscation, amputation, the language prosecutes as it describes.
explanations.C: This choice supplies an administrative rationale the passage never entertains. Standardization is nowhere offered as the institutions' motive; the stated logic is ownership announced through the pen, and the paragraph's examples are chosen for coercion, not for processing efficiency.
explanations.D: This choice imagines a freedom the examples exclude. The enslaved and the schooled were precisely those who could not refuse, and the passage's later reclamations, petitions at the courthouse, presuppose that recovering a name is a contest, not a formality.

questions.stem: An employee legally changes her name, informs her office, and finds that a senior colleague continues, months later, to use the former name in meetings and emails. Based on the fifth paragraph, the author would most likely describe the colleague's practice as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: a harmless lapse of memory of the kind long acquaintance makes inevitable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a breach of registry procedure that the courts rather than the office should resolve.

choices.label: C
choices.text: evidence that the employee's new name has failed as a name, since a name exists only for others to use.

choices.label: D
choices.text: a position taken in an argument about who the employee is, conducted through the forms of address.

explanations.A: This choice offers the excuse the paragraph pre-empts by name. Refusing someone's chosen form, insisting on the discarded name, is never a slip of memory only, the text says, and months of repetition in meetings and emails is insistence, not lapse.
explanations.B: This choice routes an interpersonal refusal into the sixth paragraph's legal machinery. The change is already legally complete; what remains is the fifth paragraph's quieter economy, where the colleague's usage is a social act no court is asked to referee.
explanations.C: This choice misapplies the sixth paragraph's structural limit to a single holdout. A name no one will say has failed; one colleague's refusal against an office that has adopted the name shows the negotiation in progress, not the name's death, and the failure standard requires the community, not a dissenter.
explanations.D: Correct. The fifth paragraph states it directly: insisting on the discarded name is never a slip of memory only, it is a position in an argument about who the person is, conducted through vocatives. The colleague's persistence is that argument in miniature, a refusal to re-sign performed each time the old name is chosen.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the third paragraph's claim that a name alone carries social information that listeners act on?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Large replications of the résumé studies finding no callback differences across name types once qualifications are truly identical.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that a growing number of employers now remove applicant names from résumés before any screening, evaluation, or callback decision begins.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that many currently popular first names are shared widely across several different social classes and regions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surveys in which listeners uniformly deny making any inferences about strangers on the basis of their names alone.

explanations.A: Correct. The claim rests on the résumé paradigm: identical materials, different names, moved callback rates. Replications finding no differences once qualifications are truly matched, and attributing earlier effects to unmatched content, would remove the claim's central evidence and with it the demonstration that listeners act on the name alone.
explanations.B: This choice reports a remedy that presupposes the problem. Employers blinding names are acting on the belief that names move judgments, which is the claim's premise institutionalized, not its refutation.
explanations.C: This choice shows some names carry less information, which the claim can absorb: the freight varies by name, and the studies' effects concern names that do carry legible signals, whose existence overlapping popular names does not disturb.
explanations.D: This choice collects denials of a process the paragraph locates beneath report. Listeners acting on names need not know they do so; the résumé studies measure behavior precisely because self-description is untrustworthy here, so the surveys cannot outweigh them.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the sixth paragraph's claim that a name's function is performed in other mouths, so that a name no one will say has failed as a name?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that most legal name changes are approved by courts without objection from any party.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that people whose names are frequently mispronounced report correcting others less and less often as the years of repetition wear them down.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that some writers publish under pen names while keeping their legal names for private life.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Studies finding adopted names never taken up by a bearer's community lapsed even in the bearers' own use, while changes embraced by others persisted.

explanations.A: This choice measures legal friction, not social uptake. Court approval settles the registry entry the sixth paragraph mentions, but the claim locates a name's life in the community's mouths, about which uncontested paperwork says nothing.
explanations.B: This choice tracks fatigue with correction, which concerns pronunciation of a name in use, not the survival of the name itself; a mangled name is still being said, so the failure condition, no one will say it, is never engaged.
explanations.C: This choice describes names deliberately partitioned by audience, each functioning in its own community of speakers; the pen name is said by readers, the legal name by intimates, so both succeed on the claim's terms and neither tests it.
explanations.D: Correct. The claim predicts that community uptake is the medium in which a name lives or dies. Studies finding that unadopted names lapse even in their bearers' own usage, while identical changes embraced by others persist, isolate uptake as the deciding variable and confirm that the name's function, hence its survival, resides in other mouths.
